Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptical negative formula; fixed interjectional clause with negative particle äb and postpositional phrase iļ mis.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: It is not a problem; the situation requires no apology, worry, or compensation.
Usage Contexts: daily speech; apologies; service encounters; family; informal reassurance
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A widely taught modern conversational formula; literally close to “not over/about anything,” used like “no problem.”
First Example Sentence: Ānda andõks, ma tulīz piški vēļõ; — Äb iļ mis!
Second Example Sentence: Kui tu äb sō tānda täna valmis, äb iļ mis.
Third Example Sentence: Ta kītiz: “Äb iļ mis,” ja pandiz kuordõ ukš kinni.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Polite imperative formula; second-person plural/formal imperative plus translative-resultative adjective jõvāks.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: You are welcome; accept it kindly; no thanks are necessary.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Palun, sīe um sinu ja sīe um jõvā.
Usage Contexts: gratitude replies; hospitality; elder–younger speech; community events
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Hospitality formula associated with giving food, help, or a favor and asking the listener to receive it well.
First Example Sentence: Tienūb sidāmõst! — Võtāgid jõvāks, se um veiti abbõ.
Second Example Sentence: Kui külāliz võttiz leibõ, perīmīez kītiz: “Võtāgid jõvāks!”
Third Example Sentence: Mēg andām sidāmõst ja kītõm: võtāgid jõvāks.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed gratitude formula; noun of thanks plus elative/ablative-like phrase sidāmõst “from the heart.”
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Deep heartfelt thanks; sincere gratitude beyond a routine thank-you.
Usage Contexts: ceremonies; family; public thanks; letters; cultural gatherings
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Built on the cultural metaphor of the heart as the seat of sincerity and emotional truth.
First Example Sentence: Tienūb sidāmõst, ku sa opātiz min līvõ kēļõ.
Third Example Sentence: Mēg kēratiz kirja ja lõppõs pandiz: tienūb sidāmõst.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Proverbial independent clause; singular subject with negative verb and adverb kunāid “never.”
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: One language is never enough; multilingual knowledge is always valuable.
Usage Contexts: education; language activism; multilingual families; speeches; classrooms
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Modern Livonian revival saying, often used to promote language learning and multilingual identity.
First Example Sentence: Skūolõs opātiji kītiz: īdkõks kīeldõ äb täut kunāid.
Second Example Sentence: Kui laps kuulõb līvõ ja latvīž kēļõ, īdkõks kīeldõ äb täut kunāid.
Third Example Sentence: Rāndas se vanā sōna minnõn alati miele: īdkõks kīeldõ äb täut kunāid.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Short personifying clause; inanimate noun rānda “coast/shore” as subject plus finite verb kutsūb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person feels drawn back to the Livonian Coast, home, heritage, or the sea.
Usage Contexts: nostalgia; migration; heritage talk; poetry; travel; family memory
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in the Livonian Coast as a homeland symbol; the shore is treated as an active caller.
First Example Sentence: Kui ma kuuliz tuļ ja mer, rānda kutsūb mindõ tagāz.
Second Example Sentence: Täm pǟva Rīgõs um pikk, bet rānda kutsūb.
Third Example Sentence: Vanaizā kītiz, et rānda kutsūb neid, kis siedā kord kuulõb.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Equative metaphorical clause; noun mer “sea” linked by copula to locative/essive heart expression.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The sea is central to identity, livelihood, feeling, or memory.
Usage Contexts: coastal culture; fishing; songs; heritage writing; reflective speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects Livonian coastal life, where the sea shaped work, travel, food, songs, and memory.
First Example Sentence: Kalāmīez kītiz vaikiz: mer um sidāmõs.
Second Example Sentence: Loulõs laps kuulõb, ku mer um sidāmõs ja rānda um kuodõ.
Third Example Sentence: Kui torm tulāb, mēg mūštõm, et mer um sidāmõs, bet ka väggi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Comparative simile; noun sōna “word” plus copula and comparison kui kiv “like stone.”
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A promise or spoken word is firm, weighty, and should be kept.
Usage Contexts: promises; agreements; family advice; community trust; moral teaching
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A coastal-community value expression: speech has social weight when written contracts are absent.
First Example Sentence: Äb lūo tüjī lubādõks, sōna um kui kiv.
Second Example Sentence: Vanā kalāmīez piddiz aigõ, sest tem sōna um kui kiv.
Third Example Sentence: Kui mēg lepīm kuodõs kokkõ, siis sōna um kui kiv.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Metaphorical clause; noun kīel “language” as subject with verb jelāb “lives” and inessive phrase suos “in the mouth.”
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A language survives only when people actively speak it.
Usage Contexts: language revitalization; teaching; activism; family language policy
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A revival-oriented saying tying living language to everyday speech rather than archives alone.
First Example Sentence: Opātiji kītiz laspõn: kīel jelāb suos, mitte ainult rōntõs.
Second Example Sentence: Kui mēg igāpǟva rõkāndõm līvõ kēļõ, kīel jelāb suos.
Third Example Sentence: Sīe um väggi tǟdõ: kīel jelāb suos ja sidāmõs.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Causative metaphorical clause; adjective/noun phrase “another word” as subject plus verb pandāb and allative/local phrase tuļt pääl.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A poorly chosen extra word makes a conflict worse.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Äb kītõ liegõ, ku rižtõl um juba pahā.
Usage Contexts: arguments; politics; workplace conflict; family disputes; warnings
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses fire as a common Baltic-Finnic and coastal metaphor for conflict spreading quickly.
First Example Sentence: Koosõmiers tūoiz sōna pandāb tuļt pääl ja kõik jägiz vaikõks.
Second Example Sentence: Äb vasta vihās, sest tūoiz sōna pandāb tuļt pääl.
Third Example Sentence: Ta tahtiz mierõ, bet tem tūoiz sōna pandiz tuļt pääl.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed well-wishing formula; adverbial/adjectival vȯndziztõ plus noun riekkõ “journey/trip.”
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Have a good trip; may your journey go safely and well.
Usage Contexts: farewells; travel; boats; buses; family departures; seasonal movement
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Traditional travel wish shaped by coastal mobility, fishing routes, and journeys between villages.
First Example Sentence: Kui paņt sõitiz Sīkrõgõ pǟl, jemā kītiz: vȯndziztõ riekkõ!
Second Example Sentence: Vȯndziztõ riekkõ, ja tul tagāz jõvā ūomõg!
Third Example Sentence: Külālizõd astiz jarā, ning perīnaiz kītiz neile: vȯndziztõ riekkõ.
